planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or plant container-grown plants in spring or fall in dry to moist, well-drained shade; white wood aster is one of the most adaptable native perennials and tolerates deep, dry shade under mature trees where almost nothing else blooms. It self-sows freely in appropriate conditions.

directions: Plant in dry to moist, well-drained soil in part shade to full shade; white wood aster is one of the very few native perennials that genuinely thrives in dry shade under mature trees — conditions that defeat most other plants — producing clouds of small but profuse white daisy flowers with yellow centers in August and September when the shade garden most needs color. The zigzag stems are characteristic and the dark green, heart-shaped leaves are attractive throughout the growing season. It spreads by both rhizomes and self-seeding to fill in a shade garden naturally over time, creating a loose, naturalistic colony that needs no staking or division. It is also an important late-season nectar source for migrating monarchs and other butterflies and hosts numerous native bee species. One of the most underused native perennials for difficult dry-shade situations under oaks and maples where most other plants decline.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or late fall directly in very well-drained soil in full sun; Eryngium amethystinum is tap-rooted and resents transplanting — direct seeding or planting small container plants while young gives the best results. Established plants are very drought-tolerant and should not be moved.

directions: Sow seeds in well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; amethyst sea holly is a European native producing the most intensely colored blue-violet flowers of any Eryngium, with small, egg-shaped thistle-like flower heads surrounded by prominent, spiky, metallic amethyst-violet bracts that color the entire upper portion of the plant in shimmering blue-purple from July through September. The metallic blue color is extraordinary — simultaneously blue, violet, and silver depending on the light angle. As with all sea hollies, the tap-rooted plants are extremely drought-tolerant and thrive in hot, dry, gravelly, or alkaline soils where they need no irrigation once established. Bees and butterflies mob the flowers throughout the long bloom season. The dried flower heads retain their metallic color and are excellent for everlasting flower arrangements. Combines beautifully with ornamental grasses, yarrows, and silvery-leafed plants.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall for spring germination or in early spring in cold-stratified conditions; seeds require a period of cold to germinate well. Sow directly where plants will grow permanently, as the deep taproot makes transplanting difficult. Allow plants to self-sow after flowering to maintain a naturalized colony.

directions: Sow seeds in fall in well-drained, lean, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; Miss Wilmott's Ghost is the most dramatically architectural of all sea hollies, named for the Victorian horticulturalist Ellen Willmott who supposedly secretly scattered seeds in gardens she visited — so the plants appeared mysteriously the following year as her 'ghost.' It produces enormously large, pale silvery-blue-white flower heads and bracts that can reach 4–6 inches across, with a ghostly, pewter-silver appearance unlike the more purely blue Eryngiums. As a monocarpic biennial, it germinates one year, blooms spectacularly the next, sets seed, and dies — but it self-sows so freely in appropriate conditions that it maintains a permanent colony. Combine with blue-gray grasses, dark-leafed salvias, or purple alliums for a dramatic effect. The dried flower heads are unsurpassed for flower arrangements.

planting.note: Sow seeds in fall or early spring in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Small's penstemon tolerates partial shade better than most penstemons and is one of the few species that naturalize reliably in the humid Southeast. It self-sows freely and forms naturalized colonies in appropriate conditions.

directions: Sow seeds or plant divisions in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; Small's penstemon is an Appalachian native producing spikes of large, tubular flowers in a distinctive combination of rosy-purple and white — the wide throat of each flower is striped with purple guidelines on a white background, giving a bicolored effect. It blooms in late spring (May–June), a slightly earlier time than many penstemons. Unlike western penstemons that often fail in humid southeastern gardens, Small's penstemon is native to the humid forest edges and rocky slopes of the southern Appalachians from Virginia to Georgia and is naturally adapted to the summer humidity and rainfall of the Southeast. It is one of the most garden-worthy penstemons for humid zone 7–9 gardens where western species typically struggle or die after one season. An important early nectar source for hummingbirds and native bumblebees.

planting.note: Direct sow seeds in early spring or fall in full sun in well-drained to average soil; starry rosinweed is tap-rooted and difficult to transplant once established. It tolerates poor, dry soils and is considerably smaller than cup plant or compass plant, making it more suitable for residential gardens.

directions: Sow seeds in well-drained, average to lean, neutral to slightly acidic soil in full sun; starry rosinweed is the most compact and garden-appropriate of the silphiums — a southeastern native prairie wildflower producing cheerful, bright-yellow daisy flowers 2–3 inches across with many narrow petals in a starry arrangement from July through September on plants that stay 2–5 feet tall rather than the 8–10 feet of compass plant. The rough, hairy, paired leaves are distinctive, as is the clear yellow of the ray flowers. It thrives in dry, poor soils where it needs no fertilization and minimal supplemental water once established. Native to dry, open woods, roadsides, and pine flatwoods from Virginia south to Florida and west to Texas, it is reliably perennial in the Southeast and Gulf Coast and grows considerably better in hot, humid conditions than many other prairie composites. Goldfinches consume the seeds in fall.
